findings.title: taskStatusData and metrics diverge when scope exceeds PROJECT_DETAILS_LIMIT (50)
findings.description: `getProjects` applies `take: PROJECT_DETAILS_LIMIT` (50), truncating the project list. `getTasks` is scoped by `projectWhere` (no limit), so it can return tasks from all projects in scope (e.g. 200). `taskStatusGroupBy` is then built from only the 50 returned project IDs. As a result, `calculateMetrics` (consuming the full `tasks` array) counts all tasks across all projects, while `getTaskStatusData` (consuming `taskStatusGroupBy`) only covers the 50-project subset. The two panels on the analytics dashboard will show contradictory numbers: the overall 'Taux de Completion' metric counts tasks from projects 51-N, but the per-status breakdown chart silently omits them.
findings.root_cause: `getProjects` hard-caps at 50 rows but `getTasks` has no corresponding cap; the shared `taskStatusGroupBy` is then scoped to only the 50 returned project IDs instead of using the same unrestricted scope.
findings.impact: Analytics dashboard shows inconsistent KPIs: the completion-rate metric reflects a larger task set than the status-breakdown chart. The discrepancy grows with the number of projects in scope. Users with >50 projects in scope will see a completion rate that does not match the sum of the status chart values.
findings.suggested_fix: Either (a) apply no `take` limit in `getProjects` for analytics (use a paginated or export path for the details table separately), or (b) derive `taskStatusGroupBy` from the same `projectWhere` clause as `getTasks` rather than from the post-truncated `projectIds`. If the 50-project cap must be kept for payload size, apply it consistently to `tasks` as well and document the sampling clearly.
acceptance_criteria: When a user has 60 projects in scope, sum(taskStatusData[].value) equals metrics[1].change numerator (completedTasks count)
Integration test: seed 60 projects, call GET /analytics, verify taskStatusData sums == metrics totalTasks
findings.notes: The PER-025 comment at line 109 incorrectly states the groupBy is 'equivalent to the getTasks relation filter'. It is equivalent only when projects.length < PROJECT_DETAILS_LIMIT.

findings.title: MilestonesCompletionResponseDto.total means 'due milestones' not 'all milestones'
findings.description: `total` is computed as `completed + overdue`, deliberately excluding `upcoming` milestones. Yet the returned DTO also exposes `upcoming` as a peer field alongside `total`. Any consumer that interprets `total` as 'total milestones in scope' (the natural reading of the field name) will derive a wrong ratio manually (e.g. `completed / total` is not the same as `completed / (completed + overdue + upcoming)`). The `ratio` itself is correct given its definition, but the `total` field is misleading and can cause bugs in UI layers or downstream integrations that compute secondary metrics from the raw fields.
findings.root_cause: The variable named `total` is assigned `completed + overdue` rather than the actual total count of all milestones; the naming is ambiguous and conflicts with the natural meaning of 'total'.
findings.impact: Consumers computing their own ratios from `total` will get inflated percentages (e.g. 5 completed / 7 total instead of 5 / 10 when 3 are upcoming). Dashboard secondary metrics or exports could misreport milestone health.
findings.suggested_fix: Rename the field to `due` (or `dueTotal`) in the DTO and return object to reflect its actual semantics. Keep `total` as `completed + overdue + upcoming` if a true total is needed. Update `ratio` documentation to clarify it is `completed / (completed + overdue)`.
acceptance_criteria: DTO field name unambiguously conveys 'milestones that have passed their due date or are completed'
Consumers cannot derive a wrong ratio by using the wrong denominator

findings.title: daysFromNow in MilestonesCompletionService uses fixed MS_PER_DAY, off-by-one across DST transitions
findings.description: Dividing a millisecond delta by a fixed `MS_PER_DAY` (86400000 ms) is incorrect across DST boundaries. On the Europe/Paris clock (used by the scheduler) the spring-forward transition produces a 23-hour day and the fall-back transition a 25-hour day. A milestone due exactly at midnight on the day after DST change can produce `daysFromNow = 0` when it should be `1` (or vice-versa). `Math.round` mitigates but does not fully eliminate the issue for milestones due within the transition hour.
findings.root_cause: Using a fixed millisecond constant for 'one day' ignores DST; the correct approach is calendar-aware date arithmetic (e.g. UTC midnight difference).
findings.impact: Off-by-one on `daysFromNow` near DST transitions (twice a year). A milestone 1 day away could display as 0 (today/overdue) or 2, misleading users. Only affects the `details` array, not the aggregate counts.
findings.suggested_fix: Compute `daysFromNow` using UTC midnight differences: `const nowMidnightUTC = Date.UTC(now.getUTCFullYear(), now.getUTCMonth(), now.getUTCDate()); const dueMidnightUTC = Date.UTC(d.getUTCFullYear(), d.getUTCMonth(), d.getUTCDate()); const daysFromNow = Math.round((dueMidnightUTC - nowMidnightUTC) / MS_PER_DAY);`
acceptance_criteria: daysFromNow is correct on both sides of a Europe/Paris DST boundary
Unit test with mocked 'now' at a DST transition date verifies correct daysFromNow value
findings.notes: Low blast radius — affects display only. The aggregate overdue/completed/upcoming counts use direct date comparison (milestone.dueDate < now) which is unaffected by this issue.

findings.title: SettingsService.remove throws unhandled Prisma P2025 for non-existent non-default keys
findings.description: When `remove(key)` is called with a key that is neither in `DEFAULT_SETTINGS` nor stored in the database, `prisma.appSettings.delete()` throws Prisma error P2025 (`An operation failed because it depends on one or more records that were required but not found`). This error is unhandled and bubbles up as an uncaught exception, resulting in a generic 500 response rather than an appropriate 404. Because `update()` already validates `isKnownKey()`, only unknown (non-default, non-stored) keys can reach this code path, but the controller does not pre-validate the key before calling `remove()`.
findings.root_cause: `prisma.delete()` throws on missing record; the method does not check existence before deleting and does not catch the P2025 error.
findings.impact: DELETE /settings/:key with an unknown key returns 500 instead of 404, hiding the root cause and potentially masking application errors in monitoring.
acceptance_criteria: DELETE /settings/nonexistent-key returns 404 with a descriptive message
DELETE /settings/:known-key still returns 200 and deletes the record

findings.title: SnapshotSchedulerService creates a Redis connection in constructor with no shutdown hook
findings.description: A new `ioredis` client is instantiated directly in the constructor, creating an additional Redis TCP connection on top of any existing CacheService or Bull connections. There is no `onApplicationShutdown()` or `onModuleDestroy()` lifecycle hook that calls `this.redis.disconnect()` or `this.redis.quit()`. During graceful shutdown or in test environments that create/destroy NestJS modules, the connection is left open, preventing the process from exiting cleanly. In tests, this can cause 'open handles' warnings and hang the test runner.
findings.root_cause: Raw `ioredis` client is created in-constructor without registering a shutdown hook to close it; NestJS lifecycle cleanup is never invoked.
findings.impact: Unclosed Redis connection on module teardown; process may not exit cleanly in prod restarts or in test suites, potentially causing port exhaustion under frequent restart scenarios.
findings.suggested_fix: Implement `OnModuleDestroy` and call `await this.redis.quit()` in `onModuleDestroy()`. Alternatively, inject a shared Redis client via a NestJS provider so the connection lifecycle is managed centrally.
acceptance_criteria: Jest/Vitest test runner exits without 'open handles' warning when SnapshotSchedulerService is included in test module
Redis connection is closed on NestJS graceful shutdown
findings.notes: This is a correctness/resource-management issue rather than a functional bug. The cron job itself works correctly.

findings.title: SettingsService.bulkUpdate performs sequential un-transacted upserts — partial failure leaves inconsistent state
findings.description: Each iteration calls `this.update(key, value)` sequentially with an individual `prisma.appSettings.upsert()`. If the N-th key triggers a `BadRequestException` (unknown key) or a DB error, keys 1..N-1 are already committed while keys N..end are not applied. The caller receives an error but has no way to know which keys were applied. This violates the expected atomicity of a 'bulk update' endpoint.
findings.root_cause: No `$transaction` wrapper around the loop; each `upsert` is committed immediately with no rollback on partial failure.
findings.impact: A bulk update with a mix of valid and invalid keys silently persists partial state changes. For example, updating `appName` and `invalidKey` in one call would persist `appName` and fail on `invalidKey`, leaving the settings in an inconsistent partially-updated state. Severity is reduced because the key allowlist blocks truly unknown keys, but a DB error mid-loop still causes the same issue.
findings.suggested_fix: Wrap the loop in a Prisma interactive transaction (`this.prisma.$transaction(async (tx) => { ... })`) so all-or-nothing semantics are enforced. The inner `update()` should accept an optional `tx` context.
acceptance_criteria: POST /settings/bulk with [validKey, invalidKey] does not persist validKey if the call as a whole fails
POST /settings/bulk with all valid keys persists all updates atomically
findings.notes: Currently the ALLOWED_KEYS guard in update() makes the invalid-key path the most likely trigger of partial failure.

verified_clean: apps/api/src/analytics/analytics.service.ts — Promise.all ordering is safe (independent queries); getProjectDetails Promise.all safe (independent groupBys)

apps/api/src/analytics/analytics.service.ts — cache key is user-scoped (userId in key), preventing cross-user cache poisoning
apps/api/src/analytics/analytics.service.ts — getActiveUsers: hasProjectScope guard correctly prevents unbounded user scan when scope is empty
apps/api/src/analytics/analytics.service.ts — progress computation: division-by-zero guarded (total === 0 ? 0 : ...)
apps/api/src/analytics/analytics.service.ts — timeEntry hours use Number() cast from Prisma Decimal; no floating-point precision issue beyond normal JS float
apps/api/src/analytics/analytics.controller.ts — role access correctly delegated to RequirePermissions; currentUser properly passed to service
apps/api/src/analytics/dto/analytics-query.dto.ts — input validation via class-validator decorators; projectId validated as UUID
apps/api/src/analytics/advanced/services/snapshots-query.service.ts — UTC day grouping via toISOString().slice(0,10) is DST-safe for aggregation purposes
apps/api/src/analytics/advanced/services/snapshots-query.service.ts — per-project dedup correctly keeps latest-timestamp snapshot per UTC day
apps/api/src/analytics/advanced/services/snapshots-query.service.ts — portfolioAverage is mean of all snapshots (not mean-of-means), consistent with spec comment
apps/api/src/analytics/advanced/services/snapshots-query.service.ts — query.from/to date parsing via new Date(string) is safe; ISO strings validated by IsDateString decorator
apps/api/src/analytics/advanced/services/workload.service.ts — UNION DISTINCT deduplication via Map<taskId> correctly handles tasks in both assigneeId and taskAssignments relations
apps/api/src/analytics/advanced/services/workload.service.ts — limit applied after in-memory sort, consistent behaviour
apps/api/src/analytics/advanced/services/recent-activity.service.ts — UTC bucket alignment (Date.UTC) is correct and DST-safe for bucket boundaries
apps/api/src/analytics/advanced/services/recent-activity.service.ts — updatedAt used as completedAt proxy is documented and consistent
apps/api/src/analytics/advanced/services/project-health.service.ts — computeHealth logic: endDate comparison uses current Date() instance, consistent within the call
apps/api/src/analytics/advanced/services/project-health.service.ts — progressPct from snapshot: Number() cast from Decimal is correct

apps/api/src/analytics/advanced/services/milestones-completion.service.ts — aggregate counts (completed, overdue, upcoming) correctly use direct Date comparison, not the DST-sensitive daysFromNow calculation
apps/api/src/analytics/advanced/services/tasks-breakdown.service.ts — exhaustive if/else chain over Priority and TaskStatus enums; no missing cases given the 4/5 enum values
apps/api/src/analytics/advanced/snapshot-scheduler.service.ts — Redis SET NX EX leader-lock correctly prevents duplicate runs; fail-open semantics documented

apps/api/src/metrics/metrics.controller.ts — METRICS_TOKEN check uses strict equality; no timing-attack risk for internal monitoring token
apps/api/src/metrics/metrics.interceptor.ts — catchError correctly re-throws original error after recording; metrics recording failure is swallowed without affecting response
apps/api/src/settings/settings.service.ts — ALLOWED_KEYS guard in update() prevents injection of unknown setting keys
apps/api/src/settings/settings.service.ts — onModuleInit error is caught and logged; startup does not fail if settings table is absent (migration not yet run)
apps/api/src/settings/settings.service.ts — parseValue catch returns raw string on JSON parse failure; no exception leaks
apps/api/src/settings/settings.service.ts — findByCategory: AppSettingsCategory enum check prevents invalid enum comparison reaching Postgres
apps/api/src/settings/settings.controller.ts — PUT :key JSON.parse in controller: fall-through to raw string is correct and consistent with service

apps/api/src/analytics/advanced/analytics-advanced.controller.ts — all endpoints require reports:view permission; currentUser correctly extracted
